coredata.dc:title: Lignin carbon dots as effective dopants and passivators for SnO₂ electron transport layers to achieve high-performance perovskite solar cells
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Fang, Dongjun
Niu, Tianqi
Chen, Ziming
Zhang, Junxian
Zhang, Ze
Zhou, Shuang
Liu, Hao
Chen, Gang
Fu, Nianqing
Xue, Qifan
Tao, Jinsong
coredata.dc:description: The low-temperature processing advantage of SnO₂ makes it a promising electron transport layer material for perovskite solar cells (PSCs). However, this advantage comes at the cost of the formation of oxygen-vacancy surface defects that hamper device performance. Here, by using bioresource lignin as precursors, we fabricate an effective dopant and passivator of lignin carbon dots to mitigate the defects of SnO2 and improve the PSC properties. Featuring the excellent compatibility and abundant surface groups of L-CDs, the defects of SnO2 are passivated and the trap-state density is reduced, the charge extraction/transfer is improved, the carrier accumulation and recombination are suppressed. These collective improvements result in significant performance improvements, including an 11.8 % reduction in trap-state density, a 251.7 % increase in electron mobility, and a 15.2 % enhancement in power conversion efficiency. Additionally, the device demonstrates improved long-term stability. This work addresses the limitations of SnO₂ and may expand its application in high-performance PSC fabrication.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lignin
Carbon dot
SnO2 electron transport layer
Surface defects
Passivation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of L-CDs and the passivation of SnO2 ETLs of PSCs
Synthesis of L-CDs from lignin and their surface state characterization
Effects of the passivation of SnO2 on electron extraction, trap-state density, and device resistance
Effects of the passivation of SnO2 on PSC device performance

Q. Xue acknowledges the open research fund of Songshan Lake Materials Laboratory (2021SLABFN17), Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of New and Renewable Energy Research and Development (E239kf0901), Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation for Distinguished Young Scholar (2021B1515020028), National Natural Science Foundation of China (22379045) and TCL Young Scholars Program.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation of lignin carbon dots (L-CDs) and their doping processes and passivation of SnO2 ETLs for perovskite solar cells (PSCs). (a) Starting material of natural wood. (b) Lignin. (c) L-CDs aqueous solution with low cost, low toxicity, and excellent dispersibility. (d) SnO2 dispersion. (e) L-CDs/SnO2 dispersion. (f) L-CDs/SnO2 ETL, in which the oxygen-vacancy defects of SnO2 were passivated and mitigated by the nitrogen- and oxygen-containing groups of L-CDs. (g) PSC with improved performance because of the mitigated defects, reduced trap-sate density, enhanced electron mobility, and suppressed charge recombination.

Characterization of SnO2 and L-CDs. (a) SEM image of FTO. (b) SEM image of nanosized SnO2. (c) SEM images of L-CDs. (d) HR TEM image of L-CDs. (e) HR TEM image of SnO2. (f) SEM image of L-CDs/SnO₂. (g) Particle size distribution of L-CDs. (h) XRD pattern of L-CDs. (i) UV absorption, photoluminescence excitation and emission spectra of L-CDs. (j) XRD pattern of L-CDs/SnO2 with the addition of 0, 1.5, 3.0, 5.0, and 10.0 vol% L-CDs.

Synthesis of L-CDs and their surface state characterization. (a) Producing procedures of L-CDs from lignin with intrinsic aromatic structure and high carbon content. (b) Infrared spectra of the L-CDs produced with hydrogen peroxide and ammonia liquor treatments. (c) N1s and (d) C1s XPS spectra of L-CDs, showing the prepared L-CDs have functional groups of C=O, COOH, and NH. (e) C 1s XPS spectra of L-CDs and L-CDs/SnO2, (f) Sn 3d and (g) O 1s XPS spectra of SnO2 and L-CDs/SnO2. The shifts in C 1s, Sn 3d, and O 1s XPS spectra imply the interaction generated between the surface O of L-CDs and the Sn of SnO2.

Effects of the passivation of SnO2 on trap-state density, electron extraction, and device resistance. (a) Steady-state photoluminescence spectra (SSPL) and (b) time-resolved photoluminescence spectra (TRPL) of the perovskite layers deposited on the L-CDs/SnO2 ETLs with the addition of different amounts of L-CDs, implying the electron extraction and transport was significantly enhanced. Dark-state J-V curves of single-electron devices with the bare SnO2 (c) and 3 vol% L-CDs/SnO2 ETLs (d), showing the trap-state density was reduced with the addition of L-CDs. (e) Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of the perovskite layers based on bare SnO2 and 3 vol% L-CDs/SnO2 ETLs. (f) Normalized transient photovoltage decay curves of the PSCs based on bare SnO2 and 3 vol% L-CDs/SnO2 ETLs.

Effects of the passivation of SnO2 on device performance. (a) Device structure based on L-CDs/SnO2 ETLs. (b) Digital photo images of L-CDs/SnO2 aqueous solution with excellent compatibility. (c) J-V curves of PSCs with different L-CDs/SnO2 ETLs. (d) J-V curves and PCE of the PSCs based on bare SnO2 and 3 vol% L-CDs/SnO2 ETLs. (e) Statistical PCE histogram of the devices deposited on different L-CDs/SnO2 ETLs. (f) EQE spectra, (g) electron mobility, and (h) stability of the devices based on bare SnO2 and 3 vol% L-CDs/SnO2 ETLs. The device performance is all improved which should be attributed to the effective passivation of SnO2 by the L-CDs.
Device performance based on different L-CDs/SnO2 ETLs.

head.ce:title: Lignin carbon dots as effective dopants and passivators for SnO₂ electron transport layers to achieve high-performance perovskite solar cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Pulp and Paper Engineering and State Key Laboratory of Luminescent Materials and Devices, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou, 510640, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Bengbu-SCUT Research Center for Advanced Manufacturing of Biomaterials, Bengbu, Anhui, 233010, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors. State Key Laboratory of Pulp and Paper Engineering and State Key Laboratory of Luminescent Materials and Devices, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou, 510640, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Pulp and Paper Engineering and State Key Laboratory of Luminescent Materials and Devices

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The low-temperature processing advantage of SnO₂ makes it a promising electron transport layer material for perovskite solar cells (PSCs). However, this advantage comes at the cost of the formation of oxygen-vacancy surface defects that hamper device performance. Here, by using bioresource lignin as precursors, we fabricate an effective dopant and passivator of lignin carbon dots to mitigate the defects of SnO2 and improve the PSC properties. Featuring the excellent compatibility and abundant surface groups of L-CDs, the defects of SnO2 are passivated and the trap-state density is reduced, the charge extraction/transfer is improved, the carrier accumulation and recombination are suppressed. These collective improvements result in significant performance improvements, including an 11.8 % reduction in trap-state density, a 251.7 % increase in electron mobility, and a 15.2 % enhancement in power conversion efficiency. Additionally, the device demonstrates improved long-term stability. This work addresses the limitations of SnO₂ and may expand its application in high-performance PSC fabrication.

Lignin carbon dots (L-CDs) were designed and incorporated into SnO₂ ETLs to effectively passivate oxygen-vacancy defects, thereby enhancing the performance of perovskite solar cells. The unique aromatic structure of the L-CDs, combined with their nitrogen- and oxygen-rich surface groups, facilitated defect mitigation, reduced trap-state density, and improved electron mobility, leading to significant performance improvements.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Perovskite solar cells (PSCs) have garnered considerable attention owing to their exceptional photovoltaic performance in harnessing clean solar energy [1,2]. Their efficiency and stability are strongly dependent on their electron transport layers (ETLs) that usually are required to have sufficient electron extraction/transport properties during light-to-electricity conversion [3,4]. Among the emerging materials for ETLs in PSCs, tin dioxide (SnO2) has become a focal point of research due to its wide bandgap, high light transmittance, low conduction band shift, low cost, and low-temperature processes [5]. Particularly, the low processing temperature of below 200 °C enables SnO2 to demonstrate unparalleled advantages for simple, scalable, and energy-efficient applications in PSC fabrications [6,7]. Despite low-temperature processed SnO2 demonstrates irrefutable advantages compared with conventional metal oxide materials. Unfortunately, it introduces unprecedented limitations, the low processing temperature causes insufficient crystallinity in SnO2, leading to the appearance of the most typical oxygen-vacancy defects [8–11]. The defects introduce trap states and capture electrons, affecting electron extraction, reducing electron mobility, increasing charge accumulation and recombination at interfaces, and deteriorating device efficiency and stability. Therefore, passivating the oxygen-vacancy defects in SnO2 is imperative for enhancing PSC performance.

Numerous efforts have been undertaken to ameliorate the oxygen-vacancy defects in SnO2 with two primary strategies: i) bulk passivation by elemental doping and ii) surface passivation by passivating agents. Elemental doping is a potent approach to passivate the defects of SnO2. Notably, Ru [12], Sb [13], Y [14], and Zr [15] have been identified as effective dopants, showcasing the capability to diminish trap-state density, enhance electrical conductivity, facilitate carrier transport, and mitigate charge recombination. But the disparity in ion size between the doped isovalent elements and Sn/O may induce lattice expansion or contraction, potentially impacting the energy band structure. Moreover, the majority of these doped metal elements are both costly and toxic, curtailing their practical applicability due to stringent regulations (e.g., the European Union's Restriction of Hazardous Substances Directive) limiting the use of toxic heavy metals in consumer electronics. An alternative approach involves the use of carbon doping and passivating agents. Carbon derivative materials such as carbon nanotubes [16], fullerenes [17], and carbon dots [18,19] demonstrate efficiency in enhancing electron mobility within SnO2 [20]. These materials offer a controllable Fermi level, cost-effectiveness, abundant raw materials, chemical stability, and electrical conductivity. But the synthesis and uniform dispersion of carbon nanotubes and fullerenes in SnO2 remain cumbersome. And carbon dots synthesized from traditional raw materials like glucose and cysteine exhibit commendable dispersibility, their surface state properties are often overlooked, resulting in a relatively modest power conversion efficiency (PCE) of approximately 20 % [18]. The nature of surface functional groups significantly affects carrier recombination. Taken together, the above doping and passivating strategies have made undisputable progress, but they are still constrained by some demerits. Such as most doping metals are expensive and toxic. The carbon derivatives such as carbon nanotubes or fullerenes are hard to be prepared and dispersed in SnO2. And the carbon dots prepared with traditional raw materials can't obtain a desirable improvement in efficiency. Thus, it is still a challenge to explore desirable dopants and passivators to mitigate the defects of SnO2 and improve the performance of PSCs.

Biomass and its carbon dots present a promising avenue for addressing the challenge of passivating SnO2. Lignin is the second largest green biomass resource in nature [21]. It is typically separated from wood during pulping processes in papermaking industry wherein it is regarded as waste and incinerated in boilers [22,23]. Lignin is an aromatic biopolymer. This aromatic biopolymer accounts for 25–35 % of the world's renewable carbon [24] and stands as a predominant source of aromatic chemicals with a high carbon content [25,26]. The distinctive aromatic structure and elevated carbon content make lignin an advantageous precursor for the fabrication of carbon dots. Using lignin as a precursor, lignin carbon dots (L-CDs) can be readily synthesized through hydrothermal routes. The resultant L-CDs offer several key advantages: 1) Cost-effectiveness and low toxicity. 2) Uniform dispersibility in SnO2 aqueous solutions. 3) Facile nitrogen doping by the proximity in atom size between N and C atoms. The surface of low-cost, uniformly dispersed L-CDs can form nitrogen-containing groups such as NH. Meanwhile, the surface states of L-CDs can be tuned into oxygen-rich groups such as carboxyl (C=O) and carbonyl (COOH) through chemical oxidation [27,28]. Electron-rich functional groups of C=O, COOH, and NH can passivate electron-poor under-coordinated Sn atoms in the oxygen-vacancy defects of SnO2 through the interaction between the lone-pair electrons of oxygen and nitrogen and the under-coordinated Sn [20]. Therefore, lignin is expected to be carbonized into L-CDs that hold promise as a suitable doping and passivating agent for SnO2.

Here, by using lignin as a precursor, we fabricated L-CDs to remedy the surface defects of SnO2 and improve the performance of PSCs. The intrinsic aromatic structure and elevated carbon content of lignin facilitate its straightforward carbonization into L-CDs. Benefiting from the remarkable compatibility and the abundant surface functional groups of resulting L-CDs, the defects of SnO2 were passivated, leading to the trap-state density being reduced, the charge extraction being improved, and the carrier accumulation and recombination being suppressed. Consequently, a significant enhancement in device performance was achieved. This research may provide a new strategy to reduce the defects of SnO2 and improve PSC performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of L-CDs and the passivation of SnO2 ETLs of PSCs
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation of L-CDs and their doping processes and passivation of SnO2 are shown in Fig. 1 . Nature wood (Fig. 1a) was taken as the starting material to produce lignin (Fig. 1b). After oxidation and N-doping, the lignin with a natural aromatic structure and high carbon content was carbonized into L-CDs through a hydrothermal method (Fig. 1c). The L-CDs show excellent compatibility with SnO2 (Fig. 1d), and they can be dispersed in SnO2 and form a uniform L-CDs/SnO2 solution (Fig. 1e). Through spin-coating, the L-CDs/SnO2 solution was coated on substrates and annealed to form L-CDs/SnO2 ETLs (Fig. 1f). The electron-rich functional groups of C=O, COOH, and NH of the doped L-CDs can effectively passivate electron-poor under-coordinated Sn atoms of SnO2, reducing trap-state density and improving electron mobility. Subsequently, the perovskite and other functional lays were deposited on the ETLs. Finally, the PSC device was fabricated (Fig. 1g). The device performance was enhanced by the suppression of charge accumulation and the decrease of carrier recombination.

The structure and morphology of L-CDs have an important influence on passivation performance. Meanwhile, the morphology of SnO2 also affects the properties of ETLs. Fig. 2 a illustrates the SEM image of FTO substrates. Fig. 2b presents the SEM image of SnO2. Fig. 2c presents the SEM image of L-CDs, demonstrating the prepared L-CDs have a relatively uniform size and excellent dispersibility, and no obvious aggregation can be observed. Fig. 2d displays the HR TEM image of L-CDs, showing the L-CDs have a size of about 15 nm. Fig. 2e presents the HR TEM images of SnO2, indicating that SnO2 has a size of about 5 nm. Fig. 2f illustrates the SEM image of SnO2/L-CDs, showing that L-CDs have good compatibility with SnO2, which is beneficial for depositing a dense and smooth ETL. Fig. 2g plots the detailed size distribution of L-CDs, showing the diameter of the prepared L-CDs mostly falls in the range of 18–26 nm. Fig. 2h is the XRD pattern of L-CDs. A broad low-intensity diffraction peak at 2θ = 23.3°can be observed that corresponds to the (002) crystal plane of carbon, indicating the prepared L-CDs have an amorphous carbon structure. The interlayer spacing can be calculated with the Bragg equation and that is 0.387 nm, which is larger than that of natural graphite (0.335 nm). Photoluminescence (PL) performance is the basic property of carbon dots. The PL performance of L-CDs is shown in Fig. 2i. The results display L-CDs have a broad UV absorption in the range of 200–400 nm. The L-CDs exhibit strong emission with the maximum emission at 450 nm under 370 nm UV light excitation. The PL performance verifies that lignin carbon dots were successfully prepared. Fig. 2j shows the XRD pattern of L-CDs/SnO2 with the addition of 0, 1.5, 3.0, 5.0, and 10.0 vol% L-CDs, implying that the addition of L-CDs has no obvious impact on the crystal structure of SnO2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of L-CDs from lignin and their surface state characterization
ce:section.ce:para: The passivation properties of L-CDs are closely determined by their surface functional groups. And the surface functional groups are dependent on the synthesis. The synthesis of the L-CDs was carried out by two-step procedures (Fig. 3 a). The lignin precursors were first pretreated with hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) then followed by hydrothermal treatments with doping by ammonia liquor (NH3·H2O). Through the oxidation of H2O2 and the doping of N, the large carbon precursors of lignin were cleaved into small segments and then into nanosized L-CDs rich in nitrogen- and oxygen-containing surface groups [29–31]. The infrared spectra of the synthesized L-CDs are illustrated in Fig. 3b. The peak at 880 cm−1 corresponds to C=C modes [32]. The peak at 1435 cm−1 corresponds to the C-N vibration. The peak at 1620 cm−1 corresponds to C=O stretching vibration. The broad peak at 3445 cm−1 is ascribed to O-H and NH2 stretching vibrations [33]. Fig. 3c and d presents the N 1s and C 1s XPS spectra of L-CDs. The N 1s spectrum shows two peaks that appear at 399.6 and 401.8 eV that correspond to CO-NH and N-H groups, implying nitrogen has been successfully doped into L-CDs. The C 1s spectrum in Fig. 3d also shows that two peaks appear at 284.8 and 288.2 eV that correspond to C=C and C=O groups, indicating the surface groups of lignin have been oxidized into carboxyl groups such as C=O or COOH. The surface functional groups of L-CDs play a key role in the passivation of the oxygen-vacancy defects of SnO2. Because electron-rich functional groups of C=O, COOH, and N-H can passivate electron-poor under-coordinated Sn atoms in the oxygen-vacancy defects of SnO2 through the interaction between the lone-pair electrons of oxygen (O) and nitrogen (N) and the under-coordinated Sn [20]. These negatively charged groups of C=O, COOH, and N-H could passivate the positively charged defects of oxygen vacancies through electrostatic interaction [34,35]. To verify the interaction, we further performed XPS tests. Fig. 3e presents the C 1s XPS spectra of L-CDs and L-CDs/SnO2. Compared with bare L-CDs, there is a shift in the peak of C 1s after the introduction of SnO2 to a higher binding energy band. Fig. 3f and g presents the Sn 3d and O 1s XPS spectra of SnO2 and L-CDs/SnO2. Compared with bare SnO2, we can find the peak of Sn 3d is shifted to a higher binding energy band, and the peak of O 1s is shifted to a lower binding energy band after the introduction of L-CDs. The shifts of C 1s and Sn 3d towards higher binding energy bands and the shift of O 1s towards a lower binding energy band are attributed to the surface functional group absorbance of L-CDs to the Sn in SnO2, implying the interaction has generated between the surface functional groups of L-CDs and the Sn in SnO2 [36,37]. With the interaction, the defects of SnO2 ETLs can be effectively passivated. The trap-state density as well as the electron mobility of the devices can be enhanced.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of the passivation of SnO2 on electron extraction, trap-state density, and device resistance
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the characterization of the surface groups of L-CDs as well as their interaction with SnO2, the effects of the passivation of SnO2 on electron extraction/transport, trap-state density, and device resistance were further investigated. Photoluminescence (PL) properties were measured to analyze the carrier transport from the perovskite layer to the ETLs. Fig. 4 a illustrates the steady-state photoluminescence spectra (SSPL) of the perovskite layers deposited on bare SnO2 ETLs and L-CDs/SnO2 ETLs with the addition of different amounts of L-CDs from 1.5, 3.0, 5.0 to 10.0 vol%. It can be found that perovskite layers based on 3.0 vol% L-CDs/SnO2 demonstrate the strongest PL quenching effects than that of the samples based on bare SnO2 and other L-CDs/SnO2, implying the samples built on 3.0 vol% L-CDs/SnO2 ETLs have the best electron extraction and transferring ability as well as the weakest PL emission properties. Meanwhile, the time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) of the perovskite layers was tested (Fig. 4b). The lifetime of the electron can be obtained and the samples of 3.0 vol% L-CDs/SnO2 show the best performance again. For the samples of 3.0 vol% L-CDs/SnO2, the results show the lifetime of the electrons in the perovskite layers was significantly decreased from 989.6 ns of bare SnO2 to 651.0 ns (Table S1), indicating the electron extraction of SnO2 ETLs was remarkably enhanced with the addition of 3.0 vol% L-CDs, which can effectively reduce trap-state density and suppress carrier recombination. As a result, we confirmed that L-CDs can passivate SnO2 ETLs to improve electron extraction and transport. And the optimum addition amount of L-CDs is 3.0 vol%. We took the samples with 3.0 vol% L-CDs/SnO2 to continue our investigations.

Fig. 4c and d presents the results of the dark-state J-V curves of single-electron devices with the ETLs of bare SnO2 and 3.0 vol% L-CDs/SnO2. The space-charge limited current (SCLC) measurement of the single-electron devices was used to explore the mechanism of conductivity enhancement. Both in Fig. 4c and d, the curves demonstrate a linear relation of ohmic response at the low voltage range. But as the voltage continuously increases, the current curves show a steep rise because of the trap filling [38]. The trap-filled limit voltages (V TFL) can be estimated and they are 0.09 V for SnO2 and 0.08 V for 3.0 vol% L-CDs/SnO2. The trap-state density (n trap) can be calculated and it is 1.27 × 1015 cm−3 for bare SnO2 and 1.12 × 1015 cm−3 for 3.0 vol% L-CDs/SnO2 [39]. The results show both the trap-filled limit voltage and the trap-state density are all improved by the addition of L-CDs. The electron-rich groups C=O, COOH, and NH in L-CDs interact with the under-coordinated, electron-deficient Sn atoms in the oxygen-vacancy defects of SnO₂ through the lone-pair electrons of oxygen and nitrogen. This passivation process effectively lowers the trap-filled limit voltage and minimizes the trap-state density.

To research the charge transfer of the devices, we performed electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) tests (Fig. 4e). Usually, low series resistance (R s) and high parallel resistance (R rec) are favorable, which are beneficial for obtaining low total resistance and high charge transfer [40–42]. R s and R rec were calculated and the results are listed in Table S2. With the addition of 3.0 vol% L-CDs, R s decreases from 11.7 to 8.7 Ω while R rec increases from 598.9 to 1078.8 Ω, indicating the passivation can significantly improve the charge transfer. Fig. 4f presents the normalized transient photovoltage decay curves of PSC devices based on bare SnO2 and 3 vol% L-CDs/SnO2 ETLs. It can be found the addition of 3 vol% L-CDs can slow down the photovoltage decay, which is consistent with the results of Fig. 4b. The above results verify that the passivation of SnO2 by L-CDs can reduce trap-state density, decrease the device resistance, and improve electron extraction and transport, which will reduce charge accumulation and recombination and lead to an improvement in device performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of the passivation of SnO2 on PSC device performance
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the effects of the passivation of SnO2 on the PSC photovoltaic performance, we fabricated PSC devices based on different L-CDs/SnO2 ETLs with the structure of Ag/L-CDs/SnO2/perovskite/spiro-OMeTAD/Ag (Fig. 5 a). The L-CDs were added into SnO2 with a volume proportioning of 0, 1.5, 3.0, 5.0, and 10.0 %. We can find that L-CDs exhibit excellent compatibility with SnO2, and L-CDs can be uniformly dispersed in SnO2 (Fig. 5b). Fig. 5c presents the current-voltage (J-V) curves of the PSC devices based on different L-CDs/SnO2 ETLs with the addition of L-CDs from 1.5, 3.0, and 5.0, to 10.0 vol%. The detailed photovoltaic parameters of the devices are summarized in Table 1 . Among all addition amounts, the devices based on 3.0 vol% L-CDs/SnO2 ETLs (denoted as PSCs@ 3 % L-CDs/SnO2) demonstrate the best performance. Compared with the devices built on bare SnO2 ETLs (denoted as PSCs@SnO2), the PSCs@ 3 % L-CDs/SnO2 illustrate increased photovoltaic parameters, with the open-circuit voltage (V OC) increased from 1.06 to 1.09 V, the short-circuit current density (J SC) increased from 23.33 to 23.76 mA/cm−2, and the average fill factor (FF) increased from 75.63 % to 81.21 %. With the increased V OC, J SC, and FF, the PSCs@ 3 % L-CDs/SnO2 obtained an outstanding photoelectric conversion efficiency (PCE) of 20.91 %, which is far higher than that of the PSCs@SnO2 (18.15 %) (Fig. 5d). Fig. 5e presents the statistical PCE histogram of the devices deposited on different L-CDs/SnO2 ETLs. When the L-CDs were added with the amount of 3.0 vol%, the PCE values mostly fall in the range of 18.5–20.5 %, which is higher than that of bare SnO2 (18.0–19.5 %) and other L-CDs/SnO2 (17.0–19.0 %).

Fig. 5f presents the external quantum efficiency (EQE) spectra of the devices based on bare SnO2 and 3.0 vol% L-CDs/SnO2 ETLs with their integrated current density plots. In the whole visible light wavelength region, the EQE of the PSCs@SnO2 is roughly all enhanced by the addition of 3.0 vol% L-CDs, which is attributed to the improvement of the electron extraction ability and carrier mobility due to L-CD's passivation of SnO2. The integrated JSC is up to 22.28 mA/cm−2, which is consistent with the JSC profile obtained from the J-V curves. Fig. 5g presents the dark state J-V diagram of the PSCs@SnO2 and PSCs@3 % L-CDs/SnO2. According to the Mott-Gurney equation, the electron mobility of the PSCs@SnO2 and PSCs@ 3 % L-CDs/SnO2 can be calculated and it is 1.16 × 10−3 cm2V−1s−1 and 4.08 × 10−3 cm2V−1s−1, respectively, showing about a three times increase by the addition of 3.0 vol% L-CDs. Fig. 5h presents the device stability of the PSCs@SnO2 and PSCs@3 % L-CDs/SnO2. After 300 h of continuous irradiation, the PSCs@3 % L-CDs/SnO2 still maintain 79 % of the initial PCE, while the PSCs@SnO2 only maintain 55 %, implying the L-CD's passivation of SnO2 also can enhance the device stability, which is likely related to the enhanced electric field stability [43].

The above results show the device performance including V OC, J SC, FF, PCE, EQE, electron mobility, and stability were all improved after the passivation of SnO2 ETLs by L-CDs. The improved performance is likely attributed to the remedied surface defects, reduced trap-state density, improved charge extraction/transfer, and suppressed carrier accumulation and recombination, which are caused by the L-CDs' passivation properties. The striking passivation properties of L-CDs should be ascribed to i) the unique natural aromatic structure and high carbon content of lignin that enable it easily to be carbonized into high-quality carbon dots. ii)the remarkable compatibility between L-CDs and SnO2 that ensures them to be produced into high-quality ETLs. iii) the abundant surface functional groups of C=O, COOH, and NH of L-CDs, which can effectively interact with Sn of SnO2. With the intriguing L-CDs passivation properties and outstanding passivated and enhanced device performance, we anticipate this research may provide a new route for the passivation of SnO2 and take a close step toward high-performance PSC fabrication.

In summary, by utilizing lignin as a precursor, we have developed an effective dopant and passivator of L-CDs to remedy the oxygen vacancy defects in SnO2 and improve the performance of PSC devices. The intrinsic aromatic structure and high carbon content of lignin facilitate its straightforward carbonization into L-CDs. Benefiting from the outstanding compatibility and the abundant surface functional groups of the carbonized L-CDs, the surface defects of SnO2 were mitigated, resulting in a reduction in trap-state density, an improvement in charge extraction/transfer, and the suppression of carrier accumulation and recombination. Consequently, a significant enhancement in device performance was observed, evidenced by a decrease in trap-state density from 1.27 × 1015 to1.12 × 1015 cm−3, an increase in electromobility from 1.16 × 10−3 to 4.08 × 10−3 cm2V−1s−1, and an elevation in PCE from 18.15 to 20.91 %. Meanwhile, the device exhibits improved long-term stability. With the unique nature of lignin and the exceptional passivation properties of resulting L-CDs, coupled with the demonstrably enhanced PSC performance, this work may offer a simple and effective way to address the limitations of SnO2 and broaden its application in high-performance PSC fabrications.

Lignin (1 g, 99 %) was dissolved in deionized (DI) water (40 mL). Hydrogen peroxide (4.5 mL) was added and kept stirring for 12 h at 25 °C in a dark room. Ammonia liquor (10 mL) was added and the mixed solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. The autoclave was kept in an oven at 180 °C for 12 h. Then the autoclave was cooled down to room temperature. The obtained solution was centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 20 min to remove large particles and the light brown supernatant was collected. The supernatant solution was filtered with a 0.22 μm nylon membrane syringe filter. The obtained L-CDs were freeze-dried. The freeze-dried L-CD powder was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C.

The traditional n-i-p type perovskite solar cells were fabricated. The Cs0.05FA0.95PbI3 precursor solution (1.5 M) was prepared in DMSO and DMF mixed solvents with a volume ratio of 1:5 and then the precursor solution was stirred overnight in a glovebox. The spiro-OMeTAD was prepared by dissolving 76 mg of spiro-OMeTAD in 1 mL of Chlorobenzene (CB) with the doping of 17.3 μL of LiTFSI solution (520 mg/mL in acetonitrile), 27.8 μL of tBP. All solutions were filtered before use. The FTO glass (1.5 cm × 1.5 cm) was ultrasonically cleaned for 30 min with ethanol and ultra-bare water and then dried in an oven for 10–15 min at 70 °C. The prepared L-CDs were added into SnO2 dispersion (Alfa Aesar, 15 % in H2O colloidal dispersion). The L-CDs/SnO2 solution was spin-coated on the cleaned FTO glass substrate at 3000 rpm for 30 s and then annealed for 20 min at 150 °C to prepare ETLs with a thickness of 30 nm. The prepared perovskite layer was uniformly spin-coated on L-CDs/SnO2 ETLs (the spin-coating processes were divided into two stages: first 1000 RPM, 10 s; then 4000 RPM, 40 s). Then the coated perovskite layers were annealed for 30 min at 110 °C to prepare the perovskite light absorption layers with a thickness of 700 nm. After the layers were cooled down to room temperature, the prepared hole transport layers were prepared by spin-coating the perovskite light absorption layer with 5000 rpm for 30s in the Spiro-OMeTAD solution. The thickness of the hole transport layers is 150 nm. Finally, MoO3 (12 nm) and Ag electrode (80 nm) were deposited in an evaporation chamber with a thermal evaporation method. The active area of each device was 0.05 cm2.

The photovoltaic performance of the perovskite solar cells was tested by a solar simulator system. The JSC was measured under the simulated solar radiation intensity of 100 mW/cm2. The V OC, FF, and PCE were also tested. The scanning rate is 0.3 V s−1, the delay time is 10–50 m s, and the scanning step width is 0.02 V. Before testing, the device was calibrated with a single-crystal silicon cell for light intensity. The EQE was measured by a quantum efficiency measuring instrument. The J-V curves of the devices in the dark state were measured by KEITHLEY 2400 digital source meter, and the bias voltage was 0∼5 V. The electron mobility was calculated according to the parameters and the dark J-V curves.

The L-CDs solution was characterized by SEM (Zeiss, EVO 18, Germany) and AFM (Bruker, Multimode, Germany). High-resolution TEM images were acquired using a transmission electron microscope operating at 300 kV (FEI Tecnai G2 F30, USA). The functional groups of L-CDs were characterized by an infrared spectrometer (Bruker, Tensor 27/Hyperion, Germany). The element composition and element valence of L-CDs were measured by X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. The fluorescence properties of the L-CDs were measured by a fluorescence spectrometer (FluoroMax-4, Horiba, American). The UV absorption properties of L-CDs were measured by an Ultraviolet–visible infrared spectrophotometer (Lambda 950, PerkinElmer, American) in the range of 200–900 nm.

Dongjun Fang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Tianqi Niu: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Ziming Chen: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Junxian Zhang: Visualization, Formal analysis, Data curation. Ze Zhang: Visualization, Data curation. Shuang Zhou: Visualization, Data curation. Hao Liu: Validation, Supervision, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Gang Chen: Validation, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Nianqing Fu: Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Qifan Xue: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Jinsong Tao: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.
The authors confirm that the data supporting this study are available in figures, tables, and schemes within the article body and ESI.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no interest conflict. They have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is funded by Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation (2023A1515011388), the construction of Bengbu-SCUT Research Center for Advanced Manufacturing of Biomaterials (20210190), and the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2018YFC1902102). Q. Xue acknowledges the open research fund of Songshan Lake Materials Laboratory (2021SLABFN17), Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of New and Renewable Energy Research and Development (E239kf0901), Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation for Distinguished Young Scholar (2021B1515020028), National Natural Science Foundation of China (22379045) and TCL Young Scholars Program.
